title: Carved beech bread board
subtitle: A round bread board with a carved raised rim, from a seasoned beech blank
excerpt: A bread board is a flat disc of seasoned beech with a shallow carved rim that keeps a loaf from sliding and provides a grip point for lifting. The carving is minimal: a series of push cuts around the circumference to produce the raised rim profile, and a light thumb-pivot cut for the rim edge chamfer. Sanded to 240 grit and finished with board butter for immediate food use.
sourceNotes: Round bread boards in beech and sycamore documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and in British treen collections at the Victoria and Albert Museum.

content.text: A beech bread board does not need a lathe. Starting from a sawn disc of
seasoned
beech, a
blank
available from any timber supplier,
push cuts
around the circumference create the recessed field that sits lower than the rim. The rim itself is left at the original disc height and chamfered on its upper edge with
thumb-pivot cuts
. Sanded and buttered, it is ready for the kitchen the same day.
What you need: wood

A sawn disc of seasoned beech, 28 to 30 cm in diameter and 2 cm thick. Sawn discs are stocked by timber suppliers and online wood-turning suppliers. The disc should be flat; run a straight edge across it before starting and reject a warped disc. Sycamore and maple at the same dimensions are equally suitable alternatives.
What you need: tools
A sloyd knife with a sharp edge. A leather strop. Sandpaper in 120, 180, and 240 grit. Board butter for finishing.
The work
Step 1: Mark the rim boundary

Pencil a circle 2 cm in from the edge of the disc all the way round; this marks the inner boundary of the raised rim. The rim zone is the 2 cm band around the disc edge; the inner field inside the rim will be carved down by 3 to 4 mm to create the recess.
Step 2: Remove the inner field

Use push cuts across the inner field of the disc, working from one side toward the pencilled rim boundary. Take thin shavings; beech is hard and the knife must be very sharp. The inner field needs to be consistently 3 to 4 mm lower than the surrounding rim across its whole surface. Check depth by running a finger across the rim-to-field boundary: there should be a clear step.
Step 3: Define the rim inner wall

At the pencilled rim line, make a stop cut perpendicular to the disc surface, working around the full circumference: press the knife blade vertically into the rim line all the way round. Then take push cuts from the inner field toward this stop cut to clean up the step between field and rim to a crisp, vertical wall.
Step 4: Chamfer the rim outer edge

The outer edge of the disc and the top edge of the rim should be chamfered so there are no sharp right-angle corners. Use thumb-pivot cuts around the outer edge of the disc to produce a consistent 45-degree chamfer, about 3 to 4 mm wide. The same chamfer on the upper rim edge (the step between rim top and the inner wall) makes the board comfortable to grip.
Step 5: Sand

Sand the inner field from 120 through 180 to 240 grit, working with the grain in each direction. Sand the rim outer face and chamfers with 180 then 240. The knife-cut rim wall can stay as-is or be sanded very lightly to 240 along the vertical face. Wipe away all dust.
Finishing

Apply board butter with a soft cloth, working it into the carved recess and all edges. Allow to stand for twenty minutes, then buff firmly with a dry cloth. The board is immediately food-safe. Re-apply board butter monthly for a board in regular use.
Variations
Carve a juice groove in the inner field: a shallow channel about 1 cm in from the rim inner wall, 5 mm wide and 3 mm deep, worked with the sloyd knife tip.
Add a carved handle tab at one point of the rim: leave a protruding section at one side and thin it to a grip shape with thumb-pivot cuts.
Care
Wipe clean with a damp cloth; do not immerse or dishwasher. Dry immediately. Re-apply board butter monthly. Beech darkens slightly with oil and use; this is normal.

items.fix: Check depth consistency with a flat card laid across the field. Mark any high spots with pencil and take corrective passes until the field is level.
items.cause: Push cuts were made at inconsistent depths across the inner field.
items.symptom: The inner field is uneven; some areas are lower than others

items.fix: Re-cut the stop cut at a true vertical angle and take a final light push cut toward it to clean up the slope.
items.cause: The stop cut was not made perpendicular to the disc surface.
items.symptom: The rim inner wall is angled rather than vertical

items.fix: Strop the knife. Try cutting across the grain at a slight skew angle; this often produces a cleaner surface on beech than a straight with-the-grain cut.
items.cause: Beech has a very fine grain that resists a dull edge. The cut is against the grain, or the edge needs stropping.
items.symptom: The sloyd knife tears beech rather than cutting cleanly
